profiles.name: Alex Chen
profiles.stress_response: Becomes more controlling; increases meeting frequency; starts requesting daily standups
trigger_points: Finding out about changes from someone else

do: Send brief status updates before they ask
Lead with recommendations, not just problems
CC them on relevant threads proactively
dont: Make decisions in their domain without informing them
Use hedging language (maybe, I think, possibly)
magic_phrases: I want to give you a heads-up before tomorrow's meeting...
Here's the data, and here's my recommendation...
I've identified 3 options — here are the tradeoffs...
communication_playbook.timing: Best: Tuesday-Thursday mornings after their 9am standup. Avoid: Monday (planning overload), Friday PM (checkout mode)

profiles.name: Wei Zhang
profiles.cultural_context: Chinese engineer in American tech company
profiles.stress_response: Withdraws further; stops responding to Slack; works alone late at night
trigger_points: Being put on the spot in large meetings
Asked to present without preparation time
Shallow technical discussions that ignore complexity

do: Send questions in advance via document or async message
Give them time to think (don't expect immediate answers)
Use written communication (they're more expressive in writing)
dont: Cold-call them in meetings for opinions
Expect quick Slack responses (they batch-process messages)
Push for verbal brainstorming (they think internally)
magic_phrases: I'd love your written thoughts on this design doc when you have time...
You raised an important concern last time about X. Can you elaborate in the doc?
I trust your technical judgment on this — what approach would you recommend?
communication_playbook.timing: Best: Async always. If sync needed: afternoon, 1:1 only, with agenda sent 24hrs ahead

profiles.name: Sarah Kim
profiles.cultural_context: Korean-American, 15+ years in big tech, executive track
profiles.stress_response: Becomes more strategic and calculated; builds alliances faster; may sacrifice short-term relationships for long-term positioning
trigger_points: Anyone going above her head without informing her first
Her direct reports embarrassing her in front of her peers
Losing organizational territory to rival directors

do: Frame your wins as team wins she can showcase upward
Give her early warning on anything her boss might ask about
Show strategic thinking, not just execution
Build a reputation she can point to as evidence of her leadership
dont: Go to her boss directly without briefing her first
Create visibility that could overshadow her
Bring problems without solutions (she has no patience for this)
magic_phrases: I wanted to brief you before the leadership sync so you're not blindsided...
This could be a great story for the org — how would you want to position it?
I'm thinking about this strategically — does this align with your priorities for Q3?
communication_playbook.timing: Best: Beginning of week, proactive briefings. For asks: tie them to her current strategic priorities. Never: right before her boss meetings without advance notice
